2014 · ECCV · Benchmarks & Evals

Microsoft COCO: Common Objects in Context

The benchmark that redefined object detection as detection-in-context: 2.5 million instance-segmented objects across 91 everyday categories, photographed amid clutter and occlusion rather than in tidy iconic close-ups — and the 70,000-worker-hour crowdsourcing pipeline built to label them all.

Tsung-Yi Lin, Michael Maire, Serge Belongie, Lubomir Bourdev, Ross Girshick, James Hays, Pietro Perona, Deva Ramanan, C. Lawrence Zitnick, Piotr Dollár
venue ECCV citations 54,107 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By 2014, recognition datasets had pushed two tasks hard — image classification (is a dog present?) and bounding-box detection (where is the dog?). ImageNet supplied an unprecedented number of images and catalyzed the deep-learning breakthroughs in classification; PASCAL VOC's 20 categories over ~11,000 images had become the detection yardstick. But both leaned on iconic images: a single, large, centered object in a canonical view. Search the web for "bike" and the top hits are bikes in profile, unobstructed, neatly composed. Models do fine on those — and struggle the moment an object is small, occluded, off-center, or buried in clutter, which is what actual everyday scenes look like.

The authors argue that real scene understanding demands three things current datasets under-served: detecting objects in non-iconic views, contextual reasoning among the multiple objects that co-occur in a scene, and precise 2D localization down to the pixel. A bounding box is a crude stand-in for location — for an articulated object, most of the pixels inside a tight box don't even lie on the object — so to measure localization honestly you need every instance of every object fully segmented, not boxed.

The numbers expose the gap. ImageNet's detection set averages about 3.0 object instances per image and PASCAL VOC about 2.3; in both, over 60% of images contain just a single object category. That is recognition in isolation, not in context. COCO's purpose: a large dataset of complex everyday scenes, with per-instance segmentation masks, so detectors can be trained and judged on objects in their natural surroundings.

// how it works

The method

COCO is an exercise in dataset engineering, and the design choices are deliberate. Categories: 91 entry-level "thing" classes — objects with clear instance boundaries (person, chair, car) — explicitly excluding "stuff" (sky, grass, street) because the goal is precise instance localization. The list was distilled from 272 candidates assembled from PASCAL VOC's categories, frequent words for visually identifiable objects, and a free-recall exercise with children aged 4–8; the co-authors then voted 1–5 on each candidate for commonness, usefulness and diversity, kept the 11 super-categories balanced, dropped any class that couldn't reach 5,000 instances, and retained all PASCAL VOC categories for backward compatibility. Of the final 91, 82 have more than 5,000 labeled instances; the 2014 release ships segmentations for 80.

Finding non-iconic images turned out to hinge on two tricks. First, pull from Flickr — amateur photos with searchable tags, far fewer iconic shots than a search engine returns. Second, and "surprisingly simple yet effective," query pairs of categories ("dog + car") and object–scene pairs rather than single categories: a search for "dog" returns big centered dogs, but "dog + car" surfaces cluttered, multi-object scenes that usually contain many other categories too. At most 5 photos were kept per photographer per short time window. The result is the 328,000-image collection rich in contextual relationships.

The annotation pipeline (Fig. 3) splits the work into three crowd tasks on Amazon Mechanical Turk:

  • Category labeling — which of the 91 classes appear? Asking 91 binary questions per image would be ruinous, so workers see categories grouped into 11 super-categories and can skip a whole group at once (no animals present → don't look for cats, dogs…). For each present category a worker drags one icon onto an instance. To maximize recall, 8 workers label each image and a category counts if any of them flags it; false positives are cleaned downstream. ~20k worker hours.
  • Instance spotting — workers place a cross on every instance of each found category (up to 10 per category per image), primed with the location from the prior stage and aided by a magnifying glass for small objects. 8 workers per image. ~10k worker hours.
  • Instance segmentation — the laborious part: trace a mask around each instance. To control cost only a single worker segments each instance, but only after passing a per-category training task whose segmentation had to match ground truth (≈1 in 3 workers passed). Quality is then enforced by a separate verification stage: 3–5 workers vote on each mask, and any segmentation without at least 4 of 5 favorable votes is discarded and the instance re-queued. Segmenting runs to over 22 worker hours per 1,000 masks.

Dense regions are handled by exception: once 10–15 instances of a category are segmented in an image, the remainder is marked as a single "crowd" region and ignored at evaluation. Five written captions were also gathered per image (deferred to a separate publication — later COCO Captions). All told, the dataset consumed over 70,000 worker hours.

How good is crowd labeling? On category labeling, the union of 8 AMT workers reaches higher recall than any single expert co-author (Fig. 4a), and recall saturates around 9–10 workers. The authors estimate that with 8 annotators over 99% of categories (not later rejected as false positives) are detected. The intuition is a simple independence bound: for an unambiguous object that a worker spots with probability above one-half, the chance all eight miss it is

P(all 8 miss) = (1 − p)8 ≤ (0.5)8 ≈ 0.004 // recall from 8 independent annotators, for p > 0.5 per worker
// what they found

The results

Scale and context. The finished dataset holds 2,500,000 labeled instances in 328,000 images across 91 categories. The 2014 release splits into 82,783 train / 40,504 val / 40,775 test images, with nearly 270k segmented people and 886k segmented instances in train+val alone; the cumulative 2015 release reaches 165,482 / 81,208 / 81,434. But the number that captures COCO's intent is context density: on average 7.7 instances and 3.5 categories per image, versus roughly 3.0 instances / under 2 categories for ImageNet detection and 2.3 / under 2 for PASCAL VOC. Only 10% of COCO images contain a single category, against over 60% for ImageNet and PASCAL — and COCO's objects are smaller on average, which makes them harder and more context-dependent to recognize (Fig. 5).

Is COCO actually harder? To check, the authors run a Deformable Parts Model (DPM, release 5) on a 55,000-image subset, training two variants — DPMv5-P on PASCAL VOC 2012 and DPMv5-C on COCO (5,000 positive / 10,000 negative images) — and testing each on both datasets. Average detection AP roughly halves moving from PASCAL to COCO, the clearest evidence that COCO's non-iconic, cluttered images are genuinely more difficult.

Model (training data)PASCAL VOC test · APMS COCO test · AP
DPMv5-P (PASCAL VOC 2012)29.616.9
DPMv5-C (MS COCO)26.819.1

Two further reads from Table 1: training on COCO helps on COCO (19.1 vs. 16.9 AP) and the COCO-trained model still wins 6 of 20 categories even when tested on PASCAL. And on cross-dataset generalization — the Torralba–Efros "performance drop" between train and test datasets — the PASCAL-trained model loses 12.7 AP while the COCO-trained model loses only 7.7 AP, suggesting that more, harder training data generalizes better to easier datasets. Finally, even when a detector returns a correct box (IoU ≥ 0.5), recovering the object's mask by projecting DPM part templates is poor — average segmentation overlap is low for every one of the 20 PASCAL categories, far short of the perfect-mask value of 1.0 (Fig. 9) — underscoring that instance segmentation was, at the time, wide open.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

Judged as a benchmark paper, COCO is oddly incomplete — and the authors say so. The only detector benchmarked is a DPM, a pre-deep-learning model already being eclipsed at publication by R-CNN (whose author, Ross Girshick, is a COCO co-author) and OverFeat. The baselines were even run on a 55k subset before the final split, and the text is explicit that "baselines on the actual test set will be added once the evaluation server is complete" and that "more sophisticated approaches should improve performance substantially." So the 16–29 AP numbers are not a SOTA reading; they are a sanity check that COCO is ~2× harder than PASCAL. Fair for a dataset paper, but it means this paper sets no real performance bar.

More striking, the metric that made COCO famous isn't in it. COCO's lasting contribution to evaluation — mean AP averaged over IoU thresholds .50:.05:.95, plus AP broken out by object scale — is never defined here; the paper says only that "a full discussion of evaluation metrics will be added once the evaluation server is complete." The protocol that turned COCO into the detection standard arrived later, with the evaluation server and API. As an eval artifact, then, the paper ships the data and defers the measurement — you have to judge it as a dataset, with its scoring machinery following just behind.

The remaining caveats are design boundaries rather than flaws, but they shape what COCO can and can't measure: it is "thing"-only (no sky/grass/road), so it cannot evaluate full-scene semantic or panoptic labeling — gaps later filled by COCO-Stuff and COCO-Panoptic; crowd regions are excluded from scoring, so dense scenes are not fully evaluated; masks are single-annotator (gated by a training task and a verification vote, but with no inter-annotator agreement reported on boundary quality); and the images come from Flickr with categories chosen by author vote and a "what a 4-year-old would name" heuristic — an implicitly Western/English-tagged notion of "common objects" that later geo-diversity audits flagged in ImageNet/COCO-style sets.

baselines

A single pre-deep-learning DPM on a 55k pre-split subset; the authors call it preliminary and expect R-CNN/OverFeat to do far better. Establishes "COCO ≈2× harder than PASCAL," not a SOTA bar.

contamination

Near-duplicate removal (GIST) plus photographer/date grouping across splits, with test labels withheld behind an eval server — sound in principle, but that server wasn't live at publication.

what's missed

The headline COCO metric (mAP@[.5:.95], AP-by-scale) isn't defined in-paper; "stuff", crowd regions, and mask inter-annotator agreement are all out of scope.

would raise confidence

An R-CNN baseline at release, the evaluation metric frozen in the paper itself, and a geographic/demographic diversity audit of the Flickr image source.

// bottom line

COCO's importance was never its baselines — it was the artifact. By insisting on non-iconic, multi-object scenes with per-instance masks at 2.5M-instance scale, it redefined "detection" as detection-in-context and handed the field a decade-long benchmark. The paper itself defers the very evaluation protocol that made it famous, so read it as a dataset contribution whose measurement machinery arrived just after.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. COCO reframed object recognition as scene understanding: 91 everyday "thing" categories, 2.5M instances across 328k complex everyday images, explicitly non-iconic — averaging 7.7 instances and 3.5 categories per image versus ~3.0 / under-2 for ImageNet and PASCAL.
  2. Per-instance segmentation masks, not just boxes, are the signature contribution: bounding boxes badly approximate articulated objects, so COCO fully segments every instance — making precise localization measurable and seeding instance and panoptic segmentation.
  3. The scale came from a carefully engineered crowdsourcing pipeline: three Mechanical Turk stages (category labeling → instance spotting → segmentation), 8-worker redundancy for recall, a training gate (≈1/3 passed) and a verification vote for mask quality, over 70,000 worker hours.
  4. The DPM baseline is a difficulty sanity check, not a result: average AP roughly halves from PASCAL (29.6) to COCO (16.9), proving COCO is far harder — while the now-canonical mAP@[.5:.95] metric and strong deep baselines arrived later with the evaluation server.
